chunk_summary: FSSAI fortification standards guide pediatric counseling on fortified staples like milk, rice, and salt to address iodine deficiency and anemia, emphasizing population-level strategies and avoiding over-supplementation.
learning_objectives: Understand FSSAI standards for food fortification and their relevance to pediatric nutrition.
Identify fortified staples commonly used in public programs like PDS and school meals.
Counsel families on the role of fortified foods in normal diets and their limitations in treating deficiencies.
Recognize special considerations for children with hemoglobinopathies regarding iron exposure.
key_facts: Fortified staples include milk and oil (vitamins A and D), rice and atta (iron, folic acid, vitamin B12), and iodized salt (iodine).
Double fortified salt contains both iodine and iron for enhanced deficiency control.
Fortification is a population strategy, not a treatment for moderate/severe anemia.
Clinicians should avoid unregulated multivitamins when families already consume fortified staples and prophylactic IFA.
Children with transfusion-dependent hemoglobinopathies require individualized advice on iron exposure.
reference_quote: Food fortification in India is regulated through FSSAI standards that specify which nutrients can be added to common staples and how products must be identified on the label.
